data.event: The Sacramento-based Konjo School of Martial Arts is founded in 1979 by David Butler. Patterned after the traditional martial arts schools of Japan, the Konjo School of Martial Arts (which translates "place where one learns perseverance") was dedicated to the traditional budo arts of bujitsu which included karate, judo, and weapons. Konjo blends the principles of dedication, hard training, and technical excellence with a culture of unity, family and mutual support.
The Konjo School of Martial Arts is started in a garage with seven students. In less than a year it moves to a large church building in South Sacramento, quickly growing to more than 70 students.
The first group of Konjo black belts are promoted. Among them are Lewis Mace, Jimmy Caruthers and Carl Grace.

The school begins a series of High Sierra summer camps called "Konjo Challenge." These popular camps bring together martial arts students and instructors from a variety of disciplines for three-day intensive instruction and cross training seminars.

Al Ikemoto joins the Konjo School of Martial Arts staff, beginning a long and successful instructional partnership with Mr. Butler. At this time a Roseville branch of the school is started at the Roseville Community Center.

The school opens the Sacramento Martial Arts and Sports Center. This facility remains open for two years and marks the beginning of serious competitive judo and karate for the Konjo School of Martial Arts.
While at the Sacramento Martial Arts and Sport Center, Mr. Butler founds Team Sacramento as the competitive arm of the school. The team's focus is on competitive judo and karate.

The Konjo School of Martial Arts and Team Sacramento move to John F. Kennedy High School. This allows the club to minimize expenses and send students abroad to compete in local, state, national and international events. The club remains at JFK High School for 17 years.
Al Ikemoto is appointed Head Instructor of the Konjo School of Martial Arts and Team Sacramento Judo.
After 16 years as a competitor, and 12 years as an assistant instructor, Brent Goodall is appointed Head Instructor of the Konjo School of Martial Arts and Team Sacramento Judo.
Team Sacramento Judo opens a new location in Roseville. Several new students and families join; quick growth requires relocation to a larger facility.

Thanks to UFC fighters James Irvin and Scott Smith, in October of 2007 Team Sacramento Judo relocates to the newly-opened Ultimate Training Center in Roseville. The state-of-the-art UTC facility features over 2000 square feet of mat space. Several new students and families join the team.

After nearly 18 years at JFK High School, and thanks to WEC World Champion Urijah Faber, Team Sacramento Judo temporarily relocates it's Sacramento branch to Ultimate Fitness' state-of-the-art training facility in downtown Sacramento.
Team Sacramento Judo relocates it's Sacramento dojo to the Capital City Brazilian Jiu Jitsu facility.
Team Sacramento Judo begins Saturday classes at Folsom Mixed Martial Arts.
Syria Rhodes becomes the first Team Sacramento Judo competitor to qualify for the US Junior World Team and competes at the 2011 Junior World Championships in Capetown, South Africa.
Team Sacramento Judo relocates it's Sacramento dojo to the Yemaso Brazilian Jiu Jitsu facility.
